things--everything that begins with an M--'  'Why with an M?' said Alice.  'Why not?' said the March Hare.  Alice was silent.  The Dormouse had closed

eness was more than Alice could bear: she got up in great disgust, and walked off; the Dormouse fell asleep instantly, and neither of the others took

The Queen's Croquet-Ground  A large rose-tree stood near the entrance of the garden: the roses growing on it were white, but there were three gardene
them, and just as she came up to them she heard one of them say, 'Look out now, Five! Don't go splashing paint over me like that!'  'I couldn't help
On which Seven looked up and said, 'That's right, Five! Always lay the blame on others!'  'YOU'D better not talk!' said Five. 'I heard the Queen say

INE.'  The Queen turned crimson with fury, and, after glaring at her for a moment like a wild beast, screamed 'Off with her head! Off--'  'Nonsense!'
his hand upon her arm, and timidly said 'Consider, my dear: she is only a child!'  The Queen turned angrily away from him, and said to the Knave 'Tur

complete our helplessness, down came Hunter with the news that Jim Hawkins had slipped into a boat and was gone ashore with the rest.  It never occur
chance if we should see the lad again. We ran on deck. The pitch was bubbling in the seams; the nasty stench of the place turned me sick; if ever a m
rels were sitting grumbling under a sail in the forecastle; ashore we could see the gigs made fast and a man sitting in each, hard by where the river
decided that Hunter and I should go ashore with the jolly-boat in quest of information.  The gigs had leaned to their right, but Hunter and I pulled

hark back again to "Lillibullero."  There was a slight bend in the coast, and I steered so as to put it between us; even before we landed we had thus
big silk handkerchief under my hat for coolness' sake and a brace of pistols ready primed for safety.  I had not gone a hundred yards when I reached

and ammunition, and things to eat, and excellent wines, there had been one thing overlooked--we had no water. I was thinking this over when there came

ulled back to the HISPANIOLA.  That we should have risked a second boat load seems more daring than it really was. They had the advantage of numbers,

.  And the next moment he and the captain had dropped aboard of us, and we had shoved off and given way.  We were clear out of the ship, but not yet a
Last Trip  THIS fifth trip was quite different from any of the others. In the first place, the little gallipot of a boat that we were in was gravely
over six feet high, was already more than she was meant to carry. Add to that the powder, pork, and bread-bags. The gunwale was lipping astern. Severa
we had gone a hundred yards.  The captain made us trim the boat, and we got her to lie a little more evenly. All the same, we were afraid to breathe.
westward through the basin, and then south'ard and seaward down the straits by which we had entered in the morning. Even the ripples were a danger to
place behind the point. If we let the current have its way we should come ashore beside the gigs, where the pirates might appear at any moment.  "I ca
uth, two fresh men, were at the oars. "The tide keeps washing her down. Could you pull a little stronger?"  "Not without swamping the boat," said he.
I tried and found by experiment that the tide kept sweeping us westward until I had laid her head due east, or just about right angles to the way we
we can lie, sir, we must even lie it," returned the captain. "We must keep upstream. You see, sir," he went on, "if once we dropped to leeward of the

It was a hard trip, with the mail behind them, and the heavy work wore them down. They were short of weight and in poor condition when they made Daw

out that made men laugh, for canned goods on the Long Trail is a thing to dream about. "Blankets for a hotel" quoth one of the men who laughed and he

finally wiping her eyes and proceeding to cast out even articles of apparel that were imperative necessaries. And in her zeal, when she had finished
still a formidable bulk. Charles and Hal went out in the evening and bought six Outside dogs. These, added to the six of the original team, and Teek
But the Outside dogs, though practically broken in since their landing, did not amount to much. Three were short-haired pointers, one was a Newfoundl
his comrades looked upon them with disgust, and though he speedily taught them their places and what not to do, he could not teach them what to do. T
ered and spirit-broken by the strange savage environment in which they found themselves and by the ill treatment they had received. The two mongrels w
worn out by twenty-five hundred miles of continuous trail, the outlook was anything but bright. The two men, however, were quite cheerful. And they w
for Dawson, or come in from Dawson, but never had they seen a sled with so many as fourteen dogs. In the nature of Arctic travel there was a reason w
Charles and Hal did not know this. They had worked the trip out with a pencil, so much to a dog, so many dogs, so many days, Q.E.D. Mercedes looked o
morning Buck led the long team up the street. There was nothing lively about it, no snap or go in him and his fellows. They were starting dead weary.
same trail once more, made him bitter. His heart was not in the work, nor was the heart of any dog. The Outsides were timid and frightened, the Insid
woman. They did not know how to do anything, and as the days went by it became apparent that they could not learn. They were slack in all things, with
and get the sled loaded in fashion so slovenly that for the rest of the day they were occupied in stopping and rearranging the load. Some days they d
than half the distance used by the men as a basis in their dog-food computation.  It was inevitable that they should go short on dog-food. But they h
not been trained by chronic famine to make the most of little, had voracious appetites. And when, in addition to this, the worn-out huskies pulled we
Mercedes, with tears in her pretty eyes and a quaver in her throat, could not cajole him into giving the dogs still more, she stole from the fish-sac
And though they were making poor time, the heavy load they dragged sapped their strength severely.  Then came the underfeeding. Hal awoke one day to
was to be obtained. So he cut down even the orthodox ration and tried to increase the day's travel. His sister and brother-in-law seconded him; but t
; but it was impossible to make the dogs travel faster, while their own inability to get under way earlier in the morning prevented them from travelli
Dub. Poor blundering thief that he was, always getting caught and punished, he had none the less been a faithful worker. His wrenched shoulder-blade,
saying of the country that an Outside dog starves to death on the ration of the husky, so the six Outside dogs under Buck could do no less than die o
rels hanging more grittily on to life, but going in the end.  By this time all the amenities and gentlenesses of the Southland had fallen away from th
for their manhood and womanhood. Mercedes ceased weeping over the dogs, being too occupied with weeping over herself and with quarrelling with her hu
doubled upon it, outdistanced it. The wonderful patience of the trail which comes to men who toil hard and suffer sore, and remain sweet of speech an
in pain; their muscles ached, their bones ached, their very hearts ached; and because of this they became sharp of speech, and hard words were first
the cherished belief of each that he did more than his share of the work, and neither forbore to speak this belief at every opportunity. Sometimes Mer
a few sticks for the fire (a dispute which concerned only Charles and Hal), presently would be lugged in the rest of the family, fathers, mothers, un
society plays his mother's brother wrote, should have anything to do with the chopping of a few sticks of firewood, passes comprehension; nevertheles
tongue should be relevant to the building of a Yukon fire, was apparent only to Mercedes, who disburdened herself of copious opinions upon that topic
unbuilt, the camp half pitched, and the dogs unfed.  Mercedes nursed a special grievance--the grievance of sex. She was pretty and soft, and had been
ivalrous. It was her custom to be helpless. They complained. Upon which impeachment of what to her was her most essential sex-prerogative, she made th
. She was pretty and soft, but she weighed one hundred and twenty pounds--a lusty last straw to the load dragged by the weak and starving animals. She
pleaded with her, entreated, the while she wept and importuned Heaven with a recital of their brutality.  On one occasion they took her off the sled
the trail. They went on their way, but she did not move. After they had travelled three miles they unloaded the sled, came back for her, and by main
. Hal's theory, which he practised on others, was that one must get hardened. He had started out preaching it to his sister and brother-in-law. Failin
and a toothless old squaw offered to trade them a few pounds of frozen horse-hide for the Colt's revolver that kept the big hunting-knife company at
ved horses of the cattlemen six months back. In its frozen state it was more like strips of galvanized iron, and when a dog wrestled it into his stoma
.  And through it all Buck staggered along at the head of the team as in a nightmare. He pulled when he could; when he could no longer pull, he fell d
of his beautiful furry coat. The hair hung down, limp and draggled, or matted with dried blood where Hal's club had bruised him. His muscles had wast
were outlined cleanly through the loose hide that was wrinkled in folds of emptiness. It was heartbreaking, only Buck's heart was unbreakable. The ma
ulating skeletons. There were seven all together, including him. In their very great misery they had become insensible to the bite of the lash or the

in such countless numbers, and in gayer or more jovial spirits, than around a dead sperm whale, moored by night to a whaleship at sea. If you have ne
of conciliating the devil.  But, as yet, Stubb heeded not the mumblings of the banquet that was going on so nigh him, no more than the sharks heeded
where's that old Fleece?" he cried at length, widening his legs still further, as if to form a more secure base for his supper; and, at the same time
sail this way, cook!"  The old black, not in any very high glee at having been previously routed from his warm hammock at a most unseasonable hour, ca
with his knee-pans, which he did not keep well scoured like his other pans; this old Fleece, as they called him, came shuffling and limping along, as
hoops; this old Ebony floundered along, and in obedience to the word of command, came to a dead stop on the opposite side of Stubb's sideboard; when,
back still further over, at the same time sideways inclining his head, so as to bring his best ear into play.  "Cook," said Stubb, rapidly lifting a
been beating this steak too much, cook; it's too tender. Don't I always say that to be good, a whale-steak must be tough? There are those sharks now
are kicking up! Cook, go and talk to 'em; tell 'em they are welcome to help themselves civilly, and in moderation, but they must keep quiet. Blast me
, take this lantern," snatching one from his sideboard; "now then, go and preach to them!"  Sullenly taking the offered lantern, old Fleece limped acr
the sea, so as to get a good view of his congregation, with the other hand he solemnly flourished his tongs, and leaning far over the side in a mumbl
-critters: I'se ordered here to say dat you must stop dat dam noise dare.  You hear?  Stop dat dam smackin' ob de lips! Massa Stubb say dat you can fi
dat dam racket!"  "Cook," here interposed Stubb, accompanying the word with a sudden slap on the shoulder,--Cook! why, damn your eyes, you mustn't sw
Den preach to him yourself," sullenly turning to go.  No, Cook; go on, go on."  "Well, den, Belubed fellow-critters:"--  "Right!" exclaimed Stubb, ap
that," and Fleece continued.  "Do you is all sharks, and by natur wery woracious, yet I zay to you, fellow-critters, dat dat woraciousness--'top dat
hear, 'spose you keep up such a dam slapping and bitin' dare?"  "Cook," cried Stubb, collaring him, "I won't have that swearing. Talk to 'em gentlema
fellow-critters. I don't blame ye so much for; dat is natur, and can't be helped; but to gobern dat wicked natur, dat is de pint.  You is sharks, sar
you be angel; for all angel is not'ing more dan de shark well goberned.  Now, look here, bred'ren, just try wonst to be cibil, a helping yourselbs fr
neighbour's mout, I say. Is not one shark dood right as toder to dat whale?  And, by Gor, none on you has de right to dat whale; dat whale belong to
dan oders; but den de brig mouts sometimes has de small bellies; so dat de brigness of de mout is not to swallar wid, but to bit off de blubber for d
demselves."  "Well done, old Fleece!" cried Stubb, "that's Christianity; go on."  "No use goin' on; de dam willains will keep a scrougin' and slappin
don't hear one word; no use a-preaching to such dam g'uttons as you call 'em, till dare bellies is full, and dare bellies is bottomless; and when dey
dey sink in de sea, go fast to sleep on de coral, and can't hear noting at all, no more, for eber and eber."  "Upon my soul, I am about of the same o
away to my supper."  Upon this, Fleece, holding both hands over the fishy mob, raised his shrill voice, and cried--  "Cussed fellow-critters! Kick up
dey bust--and den die."  "Now, cook," said Stubb, resuming his supper at the capstan; Stand just where you stood before, there, over against me, and
over upon his tongs in the desired position.  "Well," said Stubb, helping himself freely meanwhile; "I shall now go back to the subject of this steak
" said the old black, testily.  "Silence!  How old are you, cook?"  "'Bout ninety, dey say," he gloomily muttered.  And have you have lived in this w

But if it be the soul's disease, then do I commit myself to the one Physician of the soul! He, if it stand with his good pleasure, can cure; or he ca

.  "A rare case!" he muttered. "I must needs look deeper into it. A strange sympathy betwixt soul and body! Were it only for the art's sake, I must se
above recorded, that the Reverend Mr. Dimmesdale, at noonday, and entirely unawares, fell into a deep, deep slumber, sitting in his chair, with a lar

ig. To such an unwonted remoteness, however, had his spirit now withdrawn into itself, that he stirred not in his chair, when old Roger Chillingworth,

But, with what a wild look of wonder, joy, and horror! With what a ghastly rapture, as it were, too mighty to be expressed only by the eye and featur

is lost to heaven, and won into his kingdom.  But what distinguished the physician's ecstasy from Satan's was the trait of wonder in it!  [Illustrati

THE INTERIOR OF A HEART.   After the incident last described, the intercourse between the clergyman and the physician, though externally the sam
before it. It was not, indeed, precisely that which he had laid out for himself to tread. Calm, gentle, passionless, as he appeared, there was yet, w
man, which led him to imagine a more intimate revenge than any mortal had ever wreaked upon an enemy. To make himself the one trusted friend, to whom
, expelled in vain! All that guilty sorrow, hidden from the world, whose great heart would have pitied and forgiven, to be revealed to him, the Pitile
else could so adequately pay the debt of vengeance!  The clergyman's shy and sensitive reserve had balked this scheme. Roger Chillingworth, however,
his victim for its own purposes, and, perchance, pardoning where it seemed most to punish--had substituted for his black devices. A revelation, he co
By its aid, in all the subsequent relations betwixt him and Mr. Dimmesdale, not merely the external presence, but the very inmost soul, of the latter
thenceforth, not a spectator only, but a chief actor, in the poor minister's interior world. He could play upon him as he chose. Would he arouse him
the engine;--and the physician knew it well! Would he startle him with sudden fear? As at the waving of a magician's wand, uprose a grisly phantom,--

the bushes and under some blossom-covered apple-trees towards me. I turned smiling to them and beckoned them to me. They came, and then, pointing to

In three strides I was after him, had him by the loose part of his robe round the neck, and began dragging him towards the sphinx. Then I saw the hor
was not beaten yet. I banged with my fist at the bronze panels. I thought I heard something stir inside--to be explicit, I thought I heard a sound li
and hammered till I had flattened a coil in the decorations, and the verdigris came off in powdery flakes. The delicate little people must have heard
the <DW72>s, looking furtively at me. At last, hot and tired, I sat down to watch the place. But I was too restless to watch long; I am too Occidenta
inactive for twenty-four hours--that is another matter.  'I got up after a time, and began walking aimlessly through the bushes towards the hill agai
they mean to take your machine away, it's little good your wrecking their bronze panels, and if they don't, you will get it back as soon as you can a
monomania. Face this world. Learn its ways, watch it, be careful of too hasty guesses at its meaning. In the end you will find clues to it all." Then
and toil to get into the future age, and now my passion of anxiety to get out of it. I had made myself the most complicated and the most hopeless tra
'Going through the big palace, it seemed to me that the little people avoided me. It may have been my fancy, or it may have had something to do with
, to show no concern and to abstain from any pursuit of them, and in the course of a day or two things got back to the old footing. I made what progre
subtle point or their language was excessively simple--almost exclusively composed of concrete substantives and verbs. There seemed to be few, if any
any but the simplest propositions. I determined to put the thought of my Time Machine and the mystery of the bronze doors under the sphinx as much as
understand, tethered me in a circle of a few miles round the point of my arrival.  'So far as I could see, all the world displayed the same exuberant
in material and style, the same clustering thickets of evergreens, the same blossom-laden trees and tree-ferns. Here and there water shone like silve

rimmed with bronze, curiously wrought, and protected by a little cupola from the rain. Sitting by the side of these wells, and peering down into the
in all of them I heard a certain sound: a thud--thud--thud, like the beating of some big engine; and I discovered, from the flaring of my matches, th
into the throat of one, and, instead of fluttering slowly down, it was at once sucked swiftly out of sight.  'After a time, too, I came to connect th

associate it with the sanitary apparatus of these people. It was an obvious conclusion, but it was absolutely wrong.  'And here I must admit that I le
future. In some of these visions of Utopias and coming times which I have read, there is a vast amount of detail about building, and social arrangeme
inaccessible to a real traveller amid such realities as I found here. Conceive the tale of London which a <DW64>, fresh from Central Africa, would ta
s Delivery Company, and postal orders and the like? Yet we, at least, should be willing enough to explain these things to him! And even of what he kne

: that aged and infirm among this people there were none.  'I must confess that my satisfaction with my first theories of an automatic civilization an
I had explored were mere living places, great dining-halls and sleeping apartments. I could find no machinery, no appliances of any kind. Yet these p
fairly complex specimens of metalwork. Somehow such things must be made. And the little people displayed no vestige of a creative tendency. There wer
love in a half-playful fashion, in eating fruit and sleeping. I could not see how things were kept going.  'Then, again, about the Time Machine: some
the life of me I could not imagine. Those waterless wells, too, those flickering pillars. I felt I lacked a clue. I felt--how shall I put it? Suppose
made up of words, of letters even, absolutely unknown to you? Well, on the third day of my visit, that was how the world of Eight Hundred and Two Thou
. It happened that, as I was watching some of the little people bathing in a shallow, one of them was seized with cramp and began drifting downstream.
the strange deficiency in these creatures, when I tell you that none made the slightest attempt to rescue the weakly crying little thing which was dr
I caught the poor mite and drew her safe to land. A little rubbing of the limbs soon brought her round, and I had the satisfaction of seeing she was
from her. In that, however, I was wrong.  'This happened in the morning. In the afternoon I met my little woman, as I believe it was, as I was return
a big garland of flowers--evidently made for me and me alone. The thing took my imagination. Very possibly I had been feeling desolate. At any rate I
conversation, chiefly of smiles. The creature's friendliness affected me exactly as a child's might have done. We passed each other flowers, and she
don't know what it meant, somehow seemed appropriate enough. That was the beginning of a queer friendship which lasted a week, and ended--as I will t

on a miniature flirtation. Yet her distress when I left her was very great, her expostulations at the parting were sometimes frantic, and I think, al
it was mere childish affection that made her cling to me. Until it was too late, I did not clearly know what I had inflicted upon her when I left her
and showing in her weak, futile way that she cared for me, the little doll of a creature presently gave my return to the neighbourhood of the White S
.  'It was from her, too, that I learned that fear had not yet left the world. She was fearless enough in the daylight, and she had the oddest confide
at them. But she dreaded the dark, dreaded shadows, dreaded black things. Darkness to her was the one thing dreadful. It was a singularly passionate
houses after dark, and slept in droves. To enter upon them without a light was to put them into a tumult of apprehension. I never found one out of do

all, she slept with her head pillowed on my arm. But my story slips away from me as I speak of her. It must have been the night before her rescue tha
emones were feeling over my face with their soft palps. I woke with a start, and with an odd fancy that some greyish animal had just rushed out of the
are just creeping out of darkness, when everything is colourless and clear cut, and yet unreal. I got up, and went down into the great hall, and so o

I thought I could see ghosts. There several times, as I scanned the <DW72>, I saw white figures. Twice I fancied I saw a solitary white, ape-like cre
. They moved hastily. I did not see what became of them. It seemed that they vanished among the bushes. The dawn was still indistinct, you must unders
'As the eastern sky grew brighter, and the light of the day came on and its vivid colouring returned upon the world once more, I scanned the view keen
," I said; "I wonder whence they dated." For a queer notion of Grant Allen's came into my head, and amused me. If each generation die and leave ghosts

animal I had startled in my first passionate search for the Time Machine. But Weena was a pleasant substitute. Yet all the same, they were soon desti
Golden Age. I cannot account for it. It may be that the sun was hotter, or the earth nearer the sun. It is usual to assume that the sun will go on co
get that the planets must ultimately fall back one by one into the parent body. As these catastrophes occur, the sun will blaze with renewed energy; a
we know it.  'Well, one very hot morning--my fourth, I think--as I was seeking shelter from the heat and glare in a colossal ruin near the great hous
masonry, I found a narrow gallery, whose end and side windows were blocked by fallen masses of stone. By contrast with the brilliancy outside, it see
colour swim before me. Suddenly I halted spellbound. A pair of eyes, luminous by reflection against the daylight without, was watching me out of the
alls. I was afraid to turn. Then the thought of the absolute security in which humanity appeared to be living came to my mind. And then I remembered t
that my voice was harsh and ill-controlled. I put out my hand and touched something soft. At once the eyes darted sideways, and something white ran p
a peculiar manner, running across the sunlit space behind me. It blundered against a block of granite, staggered aside, and in a moment was hidden in
know it was a dull white, and had strange large greyish-red eyes; also that there was flaxen hair on its head and down its back. But, as I say, it we
fours, or only with its forearms held very low. After an instant's pause I followed it into the second heap of ruins. I could not find it at first; bu
I have told you, half closed by a fallen pillar. A sudden thought came to me. Could this Thing have vanished down the shaft? I lit a match, and, look
. It made me shudder. It was so like a human spider! It was clambering down the wall, and now I saw for the first time a number of metal foot and hand
, going out as it dropped, and when I had lit another the little monster had disappeared.  'I do not know how long I sat peering down that well. It wa
human. But, gradually, the truth dawned on me: that Man had not remained one species, but had differentiated into two distinct animals: that my grace
Thing, which had flashed before me, was also heir to all the ages.  'I thought of the flickering pillars and of my theory of an underground ventilati
balanced organization? How was it related to the indolent serenity of the beautiful Upper-worlders? And what was hidden down there, at the foot of tha
that there I must descend for the solution of my difficulties. And withal I was absolutely afraid to go! As I hesitated, two of the beautiful Upper-w
owers at her as he ran.  'They seemed distressed to find me, my arm against the overturned pillar, peering down the well. Apparently it was considered
tongue, they were still more visibly distressed and turned away. But they were interested by my matches, and I struck some to amuse them. I tried the
I could get from her. But my mind was already in revolution; my guesses and impressions were slipping and sliding to a new adjustment. I had now a cl
a hint at the meaning of the bronze gates and the fate of the Time Machine! And very vaguely there came a suggestion towards the solution of the econ
There were three circumstances in particular which made me think that its rare emergence above ground was the outcome of a long-continued underground

riage of the head while in the light--all reinforced the theory of an extreme sensitiveness of the retina.  'Beneath my feet, then, the earth must be
shafts and wells along the hill <DW72>s--everywhere, in fact, except along the river valley--showed how universal were its ramifications. What so nat

far short of the truth.  'At first, proceeding from the problems of our own age, it seemed clear as daylight to me that the gradual widening of the p

er underground factories, spending a still-increasing amount of its time therein, till, in the end--! Even now, does not an East-end worker live in su

s, the Workers getting continually adapted to the conditions of their labour. Once they were there, they would no doubt have to pay rent, and not a li
. Such of them as were so constituted as to be miserable and rebellious would die; and, in the end, the balance being permanent, the survivors would b
it seemed to me, the refined beauty and the etiolated pallor followed naturally enough.  'The great triumph of Humanity I had dreamed of took a diffe
I saw a real aristocracy, armed with a perfected science and working to a logical conclusion the industrial system of to-day. Its triumph had not bee
theory at the time. I had no convenient cicerone in the pattern of the Utopian books. My explanation may be absolutely wrong. I still think it is the
passed its zenith, and was now far fallen into decay. The too-perfect security of the Upper-worlders had led them to a slow movement of degeneration,
happened to the Under-grounders I did not yet suspect; but from what I had seen of the Morlocks--that, by the by, was the name by which these creature
Eloi," the beautiful race that I already knew.  'Then came troublesome doubts. Why had the Morlocks taken my Time Machine? For I felt sure it was they
? And why were they so terribly afraid of the dark? I proceeded, as I have said, to question Weena about this Under-world, but here again I was disapp
though the topic was unendurable. And when I pressed her, perhaps a little harshly, she burst into tears. They were the only tears, except my own, I
and was only concerned in banishing these signs of the human inheritance from Weena's eyes. And very soon she was smiling and clapping her hands, whi
could follow up the new-found clue in what was manifestly the proper way. I felt a peculiar shrinking from those pallid bodies. They were just the ha

doubt. Once or twice I had a feeling of intense fear for which I could perceive no definite reason. I remember creeping noiselessly into the great ha
occurred to me even then, that in the course of a few days the moon must pass through its last quarter, and the nights grow dark, when the appearance
. And on both these days I had the restless feeling of one who shirks an inevitable duty. I felt assured that the Time Machine was only to be recovere
different. But I was so horribly alone, and even to clamber down into the darkness of the well appalled me. I don't know if you will understand my fe
drove me further and further afield in my exploring expeditions. Going to the south-westward towards the rising country that is now called Combe Wood
had hitherto seen. It was larger than the largest of the palaces or ruins I knew, and the facade had an Oriental look: the face of it having the lust
ain. This difference in aspect suggested a difference in use, and I was minded to push on and explore. But the day was growing late, and I had come up
day, and I returned to the welcome and the caresses of little Weena. But next morning I perceived clearly enough that my curiosity regarding the Pala
dreaded. I resolved I would make the descent without further waste of time, and started out in the early morning towards a well near the ruins of gra
and look downward, she seemed strangely disconcerted. "Good-bye, little Weena," I said, kissing her; and then putting her down, I began to feel over
courage might leak away! At first she watched me in amazement. Then she gave a most piteous cry, and running to me, she began to pull at me with her
roughly, and in another moment I was in the throat of the well. I saw her agonized face over the parapet, and smiled to reassure her. Then I had to lo
two hundred yards. The descent was effected by means of metallic bars projecting from the sides of the well, and these being adapted to the needs of
the bars bent suddenly under my weight, and almost swung me off into the blackness beneath. For a moment I hung by one hand, and after that experienc
er descent with as quick a motion as possible. Glancing upward, I saw the aperture, a small blue disk, in which a star was visible, while little Weena
that little disk above was profoundly dark, and when I looked up again Weena had disappeared.  'I was in an agony of discomfort. I had some thought o
I continued to descend. At last, with intense relief, I saw dimly coming up, a foot to the right of me, a slender loophole in the wall. Swinging myse
rest. It was not too soon. My arms ached, my back was cramped, and I was trembling with the prolonged terror of a fall. Besides this, the unbroken da
down the shaft.  'I do not know how long I lay. I was roused by a soft hand touching my face. Starting up in the darkness I snatched at my matches an
seen above ground in the ruin, hastily retreating before the light. Living, as they did, in what appeared to me impenetrable darkness, their eyes wer
way. I have no doubt they could see me in that rayless obscurity, and they did not seem to have any fear of me apart from the light. But, so soon as
, from which their eyes glared at me in the strangest fashion.  'I tried to call to them, but the language they had was apparently different from that
before exploration was even then in my mind. But I said to myself, "You are in for it now," and, feeling my way along the tunnel, I found the noise o
, and striking another match, saw that I had entered a vast arched cavern, which stretched into utter darkness beyond the range of my light. The view
Great shapes like big machines rose out of the dimness, and cast grotesque black shadows, in which dim spectral Morlocks sheltered from the glare. Th
down the central vista was a little table of white metal, laid with what seemed a meal. The Morlocks at any rate were carnivorous! Even at the time,
inct: the heavy smell, the big unmeaning shapes, the obscene figures lurking in the shadows, and only waiting for the darkness to come at me again! Th
'I have thought since how particularly ill-equipped I was for such an experience. When I had started with the Time Machine, I had started with the ab
medicine, without anything to smoke--at times I missed tobacco frightfully--even without enough matches. If only I had thought of a Kodak! I could ha
stood there with only the weapons and the powers that Nature had endowed me with--hands, feet, and teeth; these, and four safety-matches that still r
my last glimpse of light I discovered that my store of matches had run low. It had never occurred to me until that moment that there was any need to
, as I say, I had four left, and while I stood in the dark, a hand touched mine, lank fingers came feeling over my face, and I was sensible of a pecul
about me. I felt the box of matches in my hand being gently disengaged, and other hands behind me plucking at my clothing. The sense of these unseen
to me very vividly in the darkness. I shouted at them as loudly as I could. They started away, and then I could feel them approaching me again. They
antly. This time they were not so seriously alarmed, and they made a queer laughing noise as they came back at me. I will confess I was horribly frigh
icker with a scrap of paper from my pocket, I made good my retreat to the narrow tunnel. But I had scarce entered this when my light was blown out and
after me.  'In a moment I was clutched by several hands, and there was no mistaking that they were trying to haul me back. I struck another light, an
chinless faces and great, lidless, pinkish-grey eyes!--as they stared in their blindness and bewilderment. But I did not stay to look, I promise you:
through when I reached the opening into the shaft. I lay down on the edge, for the throb of the great pump below made me giddy. Then I felt sideways
ged backward. I lit my last match ... and it incontinently went out. But I had my hand on the climbing bars now, and, kicking violently, I disengaged
ering and blinking up at me: all but one little wretch who followed me for some way, and well-nigh secured my boot as a trophy.  'That climb seemed in
I had the greatest difficulty in keeping my hold. The last few yards was a frightful struggle against this faintness. Several times my head swam, and

passages.text: blew up, the pine trees all about the guns flashed into fire, and only one or two of the men who were already running over the crest of the hill esca
they remained absolutely stationary for the next half hour.  The Martian who had been overthrown crawled tediously out of his hood, a small brown fig
had finished, for his cowl was then seen above the trees again.  It was a few minutes past nine that night when these three sentinels were joined by
seven proceeded to distribute themselves at equal distances along a curved line between St. George's Hill, Weybridge, and the village of Send, southw
batteries about Ditton and Esher.  At the same time four of their fighting machines, similarly armed with tubes, crossed the river, and two of them,
runs northward out of Halliford.  They moved, as it seemed to us, upon a cloud, for a milky mist covered the fields and rose to a third of their heig
it was no good running from a Martian, and I turned aside and crawled through dewy nettles and brambles into the broad ditch by the side of the road.
to us standing and facing Sunbury, the remoter being a grey indistinctness towards the evening star, away towards Staines.  The occasional howling of
It was a crescent with twelve miles between its horns.  Never since the devising of gunpowder was the beginning of a battle so still.  To us and to
the darkling night, lit only as it was by the slender moon, the stars, the afterglow of the daylight, and the ruddy glare from St. George's Hill and
Ditton, Esher, Ockham, behind hills and woods south of the river, and across the flat grass meadows to the north of it, wherever a cluster of trees o
through the night and vanished, and the spirit of all those watching batteries rose to a tense expectation.  The Martians had but to advance into the
explode into a thunderous fury of battle.  No doubt the thought that was uppermost in a thousand of those vigilant minds, even as it was uppermost in
were organized, disciplined, working together?  Or did they interpret our spurts of fire, the sudden stinging of our shells, our steady investment of
exterminate us?  (At that time no one knew what food they needed.)  A hundred such questions struggled together in my mind as I watched that vast sent
Had they prepared pitfalls? Were the powder mills at Hounslow ready as a snare?  Would the Londoners have the heart and courage to make a greater Mo
ching and peering through the hedge, came a sound like the distant concussion of a gun.  Another nearer, and then another.  And then the Martian besid
towards Staines answered him.  There was no flash, no smoke, simply that loaded detonation.  I was so excited by these heavy minute-guns following on
Sunbury.  As I did so a second report followed, and a big projectile hurtled overhead towards Hounslow.  I expected at least to see smoke or fire, or
, and the white mist spreading wide and low beneath. And there had been no crash, no answering explosion.  The silence was restored; the minute length
I.  A bat flickered by and vanished.  A distant tumult of shouting began and ceased.  I looked again at the Martian, and saw he was now moving eastwa
hidden battery to spring upon him; but the evening calm was unbroken.  The figure of the Martian grew smaller as he receded, and presently the mist a
dark appearance, as though a conical hill had suddenly come into being there, hiding our view of the farther country; and then, remoter across the ri
Moved by a sudden thought, I looked northward, and there I perceived a third of these cloudy black kopjes had risen.  Everything had suddenly become v
, and then the air quivered again with the distant thud of their guns.  But the earthly artillery made no reply.  Now at the time we could not underst
Each of the Martians, standing in the great crescent I have described, had discharged, by means of the gunlike tube he carried, a huge canister over
.  Some fired only one of these, some two--as in the case of the one we had seen; the one at Ripley is said to have discharged no fewer than five at t
disengaged an enormous volume of heavy, inky vapour, coiling and pouring upward in a huge and ebony cumulus cloud, a gaseous hill that sank and sprea
ungent wisps, was death to all that breathes.  It was heavy, this vapour, heavier than the densest smoke, so that, after the first tumultuous uprush a
liquid than gaseous, abandoning the hills, and streaming into the valleys and ditches and watercourses even as I have heard the carbonic-acid gas tha
surface would be instantly covered with a powdery scum that sank slowly and made way for more.  The scum was absolutely insoluble, and it is a strang
The vapour did not diffuse as a true gas would do.  It hung together in banks, flowing sluggishly down the <DW72> of the land and driving reluctantly
the earth in the form of dust. Save that an unknown element giving a group of four lines in the blue of the spectrum is concerned, we are still entir
closely to the ground, even before its precipitation, that fifty feet up in the air, on the roofs and upper stories of high houses and on great trees
who escaped at the former place tells a wonderful story of the strangeness of its coiling flow, and how he looked down from the church spire and saw
y, starving and sun-scorched, the earth under the blue sky and against the prospect of the distant hills a velvet-black expanse, with red roofs, green
rising here and there into the sunlight.  But that was at Street Cobham, where the black vapour was allowed to remain until it sank of its own accord
ading into it and directing a jet of steam upon it.  This they did with the vapour banks near us, as we saw in the starlight from the window of a dese
Richmond Hill and Kingston Hill going to and fro, and about eleven the windows rattled, and we heard the sound of the huge siege guns that had been pu
at Hampton and Ditton, and then the pale beams of the electric light vanished, and were replaced by a bright red glow.  Then the fourth cylinder fell
hills began, there was a fitful cannonade far away in the southwest, due, I believe, to guns being fired haphazard before the black vapour could over
Martians spread this strange stifling vapour over the Londonward country.  The horns of the crescent slowly moved apart, until at last they formed a
St. George's Hill was brought down, did they give the artillery the ghost of a chance against them.  Wherever there was a possibility of guns being l
Heat-Ray was brought to bear.  By midnight the blazing trees along the <DW72>s of Richmond Park and the glare of Kingston Hill threw their light upon
And through this two Martians slowly waded, and turned their hissing steam jets this way and that.  They were sparing of the Heat-Ray that night, ei
to crush and overawe the opposition they had aroused.  In the latter aim they certainly succeeded.  Sunday night was the end of the organised opposit
of the torpedo-boats and destroyers that had brought their quick-firers up the Thames refused to stop, mutinied, and went down again.  The only offen
frantic and spasmodic.  One has to imagine, as well as one may, the fate of those batteries towards Esher, waiting so tensely in the twilight.  Survi
the gunners ready, the ammunition piled to hand, the limber gunners with their horses and waggons, the groups of civilian spectators standing as near
then the dull resonance of the shots the Martians fired, and the clumsy projectile whirling over the trees and houses and smashing amid the neighbour
long, towering heavenward, turning the twilight to a palpable darkness, a strange and horrible antagonist of vapour striding upon its victims, men and

the prize, and returned with it to my hovel. Fortunately the books were written in the language the elements of which I had acquired at the cottage;
Werter_. The possession of these treasures gave me extreme delight; I now continually studied and exercised my mind upon these histories, whilst my fr
and feelings, that sometimes raised me to ecstacy, but more frequently sunk me into the lowest dejection. In the _Sorrows of Werter_, besides the int
what had hitherto been to me obscure subjects, that I found in it a never-ending source of speculation and astonishment. The gentle and domestic mann
ors, and with the wants which were for ever alive in my own bosom. But I thought Werter himself a more divine being than I had ever beheld or imagined
to fill me with wonder. I did not pretend to enter into the merits of the case, yet I inclined towards the opinions of the hero, whose extinction I w
I found myself similar, yet at the same time strangely unlike the beings concerning whom I read, and to whose conversation I was a listener. I sympat
path of my departure was free;' and there was none to lament my annihilation. My person was hideous, and my stature gigantic: what did this mean? Who
was unable to solve them.  "The volume of _Plutarch's Lives_ which I possessed, contained the histories of the first founders of the ancient republic
Werter's imaginations despondency and gloom: but Plutarch taught me high thoughts; he elevated me above the wretched sphere of my own reflections, to
kingdoms, wide extents of country, mighty rivers, and boundless seas. But I was perfectly unacquainted with towns, and large assemblages of men. The
ier scenes of action. I read of men concerned in public affairs governing or massacring their species. I felt the greatest ardour for virtue rise with
them, to pleasure and pain alone. Induced by these feelings, I was of course led to admire peaceable law-givers, Numa, Solon, and Lycurgus, in prefere
a firm hold on my mind; perhaps, if my first introduction to humanity had been made by a young soldier, burning for glory and slaughter, I should hav
, as I had read the other volumes which had fallen into my hands, as a true history. It moved every feeling of wonder and awe, that the picture of an
to my own. Like Adam, I was created apparently united by no link to any other being in existence; but his state was far different from mine in every o
his Creator; he was allowed to converse with, and acquire knowledge from beings of a superior nature: but I was wretched, helpless, and alone. Many t
protectors, the bitter gall of envy rose within me.  "Another circumstance strengthened and confirmed these feelings. Soon after my arrival in the ho
that I was able to decypher the characters in which they were written, I began to study them with diligence. It was your journal of the four months t
with accounts of domestic occurrences. You, doubtless, recollect these papers. Here they are. Every thing is related in them which bears reference to
and loathsome person is given, in language which painted your own horrors, and rendered mine ineffaceable. I sickened as I read. 'Hateful day when I
even you turned from me in disgust? God in pity made man beautiful and alluring, after his own image; but my form is a filthy type of your's, more ho
I am solitary and detested.'  "These were the reflections of my hours of despondency and solitude; but when I contemplated the virtues of the cottage
admiration of their virtues, they would compassionate me, and overlook my personal deformity. Could they turn from their door one, however monstrous,
decide my fate. I postponed this attempt for some months longer; for the importance attached to its success inspired me with a dread lest I should fa
should have added to my wisdom.  "Several changes, in the mean time, took place in the cottage. The presence of Safie diffused happiness among its in
amusement and conversation, and were assisted in their labours by servants. They did not appear rich, but they were contented and happy; their feelin
was. I cherished hope, it is true; but it vanished, when I beheld my person reflected in water, or my shadow in the moon-shine, even as that frail im
for the trial which in a few months I resolved to undergo; and sometimes I allowed my thoughts, unchecked by reason, to ramble in the fields of Parad
ances breathed smiles of consolation. But it was all a dream: no Eve soothed my sorrows, or shared my thoughts; I was alone. I remembered Adam's suppl
